mitigation_requirements: Expose deletion and temporary-use controls in onboarding
chair_output.rationale: Opt-in beta reduces trust and narrative cascade risk while preserving enough adoption signal for learning.
chair_output.strongest_dissent: Default-on could win adoption faster if controls are exceptionally clear and onboarding builds trust.
what_would_change_the_verdict: Public evidence that users strongly prefer default-on memory

claim_refs.text: The Verdict Council treated raw MiroFish report claims as substrate and used them only when corroborated by DecisionRisk metrics or run traces.

role_outputs.analysis: Opt-in beta preserves learning velocity while avoiding the adoption-at-all-costs risk of default-on launch.
role_outputs.dissent: Default-on could win adoption faster if controls are exceptionally clear and onboarding builds trust.

role_outputs.analysis: Default-on memory creates avoidable trust and press-framing risk; explicit controls are required before broader launch.
role_outputs.dissent: Enterprise-only launch may be safer for reputation but delays consumer learning.

role_outputs.analysis: The lower-risk path is a constrained launch with visible commitments, deletion controls, and enough telemetry to close evidence gaps.
role_outputs.dissent: A public launch could become acceptable if evidence closes consent and deletion-control gaps before release.
mitigation_requirements: Expose deletion and temporary-use controls in onboarding

role_outputs.analysis: Competitors can attack default-on memory as careless handling of personal context; opt-in beta reduces that attack surface.
role_outputs.dissent: Competitor attack risk may be tolerable if the company preempts the narrative with strong user-control proof.
mitigation_requirements: Pre-brief competitive narrative risks with evidence-backed control messaging
